content.text:
**README.md Summary (current contents)** ``` https://mariozechner.at/posts/2025-11-30-pi-coding-agent/ https://lucumr.pocoo.org/2026/1/31/pi/ ``` The file simply contains two external links – one to a blog post introducing the Pi coding‑agent, and another to a “Pi” reference page. There is no project description, installation instructions, usage examples, or contribution guidelines. --- ### What the README should ideally include | Section | Purpose | Suggested content | |--------|---------|-------------------| | **Project Title & Tagline** | Quick identifier | “Pi Coding Agent – a lightweight, extensible code‑assistant for VS Code/CLI.” | | **Overview** | What it does | “Automates code review, refactoring, and generation by leveraging LLMs, with a plug‑in architecture.” | | **Features** | Highlight key capabilities | • LLM‑powered code completion
• File‑system navigation
• Custom tool integration
• TUI & VS Code support | | **Installation** | How to get it running | ```bash\nnpm install -g @mariozechner/pi-coding-agent\n``` or instructions for Docker/WSL. | | **Quick Start** | Minimal working example | “Open a folder, run `pi start`, then type `# review` in the prompt.” | | **Configuration** | Where to customize | Explain `pi.toml` or environment variables for model selection, token limits, etc. | | **Extending** | How to add tools or skills | Link to `docs/extensions.md` and a brief code snippet. | | **Contributing** | How to contribute | Link to `CONTRIBUTING.md`. | | **License** | Legal info | MIT or similar. | | **Acknowledgements** | Credits | Mention any open‑source dependencies. | --- ### Suggested rewrite ```md # Pi Coding Agent Pi is a lightweight, extensible coding assistant that runs locally or in the cloud. It can read, edit, and generate code, execute shell commands, and integrate custom tools via a simple plugin API. ## Features - **LLM powered** – Uses your chosen model (OpenAI, Anthropic, local LLMs) to understand and modify code. - **File‑system operations** – `read`, `edit`, `write`, `bash`. - **Plug‑in architecture** – Add new skills or tools without touching the core. - **TUI & VS Code support** – Works in the terminal or as a VS Code extension. - **Cross‑platform** – Works on Linux, macOS, and Windows (WSL). ## Quick start ```bash npm i -g @mariozechner/pi-coding-agent pi init # create a project folder cd my-code pi start # launch the assistant ``` Then in the terminal type `# review` to ask Pi to review the current file. ## Configuration Pi reads a `pi.toml` file in the project root. Example: ```toml model = "gpt-4o-mini" timeout = 120 tools = ["bash", "read", "write"] ``` See [docs/configuration.md](docs/configuration.md) for details. ## Extending Pi Create a new tool by implementing the `Tool` interface and placing it in `plugins/`. Refer to the [Extensions guide](docs/extensions.md) for a full walkthrough. ## Documentation - [Getting started](docs/README.md) - [TUI API](docs/tui.md) - [Skills & prompt templates](docs/skills.md) - [SDK integration](docs/sdk.md) - [Custom providers](docs/custom-provider.md) ## Contributing Pull requests are welcome! See [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) for guidelines. ## License MIT © Mario Z. Czechner --- **Links** - Blog post: https://mariozechner.at/posts/2025-11-30-pi-coding-agent/ - Pi reference: https://lucumr.pocoo.org/2026/1/31/pi/ ``` --- ### Why this matters * **Clarity** – New users can instantly understand what Pi is and how to use it. * **Onboarding** – Installation and quick‑start sections reduce friction. * **Community** – Explicit contribution and license sections invite collaboration. * **Documentation linkage** – Directs readers to deeper guides without cluttering the root README. Feel free to let me know if you'd like me to implement this updated README or add any specific sections.
